name: Money as Decimals — Rupees and Paise
whatIsIt: Money is the decimal system you already use every day! One rupee = 100 paise, so paise are HUNDREDTHS of a rupee. ₹2.50 means 2 whole rupees and 50 paise (50/100 of a rupee). The price tag's dot is a real decimal point.
whyNeeded: Every shop bill, price tag, and pocket-money count uses decimal money. Master it and you can add bills, give change, compare prices, and never be confused at a shop again. Money also makes decimals FEEL real — hundredths you can hold in your hand!
realLifeUses: Reading price tags: ₹35.50 for the notebook
Adding a bill: ₹12.50 + ₹8.25 at the school canteen
Checking change: paid ₹50 for a ₹42.75 toy — how much back?
Comparing: which juice is cheaper, ₹22.50 or ₹22.05?

vocabulary.meaning: Hundredths of a rupee: 100 paise = ₹1, so 1 paisa = ₹0.01.
The SECOND seat right of the dot — money always uses two seats.

story.title: Change at the Stationery Shop
story.text: Anu buys a pencil for ₹2.50 and pays with a ₹5 note. 'How much change?' The shopkeeper counts up: 'From ₹2.50, fifty paise makes ₹3, then ₹2 more makes ₹5. So your change is ₹2.50.' Anu sees how the dot keeps rupees and paise each in their own seat.
story.extractedProblem: A pencil costs ₹2.50. Anu pays with ₹5. How much change does she get?

visual.caption: The money you pay splits into two parts: what the item COSTS and the CHANGE you get back. Cost + change = paid.

teachingGallery.title: Money — rupees and paise
teachingGallery.note: ₹ before the dot are rupees; the 2 digits after are paise (out of 100).

standardMethod.summary: Write money with TWO digits after the dot, line up the dots, then add or subtract like normal numbers.
steps: Always give paise two seats: ₹2.5 is written ₹2.50, ₹7 is ₹7.00.
To add or subtract: line the DOTS under each other — that lines up every place.
Add/subtract right to left as usual (paise first), regrouping just like before.
Put the dot straight down into the answer: ₹12.50 + ₹8.25 = ₹20.75.

mentalMathMethod.name: Shopkeeper's count-up change
mentalMathMethod.whenToUse: Giving or checking change without paper — the real-shop way.
steps: Start at the price and climb to the money paid.
Climb to the next whole rupee first, then jump whole rupees.
mentalMathMethod.example: Paid ₹20 for a ₹16.50 tiffin: 16.50→17 is 0.50, 17→20 is 3 → change ₹3.50.

alternateMethods.name: Coins-and-notes method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To make any money problem touchable.
steps: Build each amount with play notes (₹10, ₹1) and coins (50p, 25p, 10p).
Combine or remove the physical money.
Count what remains — notes first, then coins.

workedExamples.problem: Canteen bill: a sandwich for ₹12.50 and juice for ₹8.25. What is the total?
steps: Write with dots lined up: 12.50 over 8.25.

workedExamples.problem: Ravi pays ₹50 for a toy costing ₹42.75. He computes change as ₹8.75 'because 50 − 42 = 8 and keep the 75'. Check him with the count-up method!
steps: Count up from ₹42.75: to reach ₹43 needs 25 paise.
Ravi forgot: the 75 paise must be climbed OVER, not copied over.

trickPractice.intro: Rupees before the dot, paise after — and paise always fill two decimal places.

hintLadder: Paise go after the dot — always 2 digits (out of 100).
Paise go after the dot — always 2 digits (out of 100).
Paise go after the dot — always 2 digits (out of 100).
Paise go after the dot — always 2 digits (out of 100).
Paise go after the dot — always 2 digits (out of 100).
Paise go after the dot — always 2 digits (out of 100).

commonMistakes.mistake: Subtracting only rupees and copying the paise: ₹50 − ₹42.75 = ₹8.75.
commonMistakes.fix: Paise subtract too! ₹50.00 − ₹42.75 needs borrowing, or use the shopkeeper's count-up: 42.75 → 43 → 50 gives ₹7.25.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding ₹12.50 + ₹8.25 with the digits misaligned, mixing paise with rupees.
commonMistakes.fix: Line up the DOTS first — dot under dot puts rupees under rupees and paise under paise. Then it's ordinary column adding.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing two rupees fifty paise as ₹2.5 or even ₹2.05.
commonMistakes.fix: Money always wears TWO paise digits: fifty paise = .50, five paise = .05. ₹2.50 and ₹2.05 are very different amounts!

easy.q: A chocolate costs ₹5.50 and a toffee ₹2.25. Total bill? (Write like 7.75)
hintLadder: Rupees: 5 + 2 = ? Put the dot straight down.

easy.q: How do you write 'two rupees and five paise' as a decimal?
hintLadder: Paise get TWO seats after the dot.

medium.q: You pay ₹20 for a tiffin costing ₹16.50. Count up like a shopkeeper — how much change? (Write like 3.50)
hintLadder: Climb from 16.50 to the next whole rupee: how much?
Rupees tie at 22 — compare the paise.
Five paise is far less than fifty!

challenge.q: Ravi strikes again: he says ₹50 − ₹42.75 = ₹8.75. Count up and give the TRUE change. (Write like 7.25)
challenge.explain: The paise must be climbed over, not copied: change is ₹7.25.

challenge.q: Pocket money maths: you have ₹15.50, earn ₹10.25 more, then spend ₹12.00. How much is left? (Write like 13.75)

questions.q: One rupee equals how many paise?
hintLadder: That's why paise take TWO seats after the dot.
Hundredths need a hundred to make a whole.

questions.q: Change check: paid ₹10 for a ₹6.50 juice. How much back? (Write like 3.50)
Before adding two money amounts on paper, the golden setup rule is…

options.label: Line up the dots — dot under dot
Line up the last digits, whatever they are

questions.answer: Line up the dots — dot under dot
hintLadder: What keeps rupees under rupees and paise under paise?
The decimal points in a straight column.

teachBackPrompt: You're the Grove Shop cashier today! Teach someone at home the shopkeeper's count-up trick for change (use ₹50 paid for ₹42.75), and explain why ₹2.50 and ₹2.05 are different amounts.

revisionCard.summary: Money = decimals you can hold! ₹1 = 100 paise, paise are hundredths, always TWO digits after the dot (₹2.50 ≠ ₹2.05). Adding: line up the DOTS. Change: count UP like a shopkeeper (42.75→43→50 = ₹7.25).

gameMission.title: The Grove Shop Counter
gameMission.brief: You're on the till! Total the bills, count up the change, and spot Ravi's copied-paise trick before customers get short-changed. Serve 10 happy customers to earn the Golden Coin.

realLifeProject: Be the family cashier: at the next shop visit (or a pretend home shop), check the change using count-up BEFORE the shopkeeper hands it over. Report back: was it correct?

rememberIt.hook: Money is decimals: rupees.paise, two places after the dot
rememberIt.unpack: 12.50 = 12 rupees, 50 paise. Line up the dots to add. 100 paise = 1 rupee.

studentQuestions.q: Why two digits after the dot for money?
studentQuestions.a: Paise are hundredths of a rupee - two decimal places. 5.05 is five rupees and five paise.
Line up the decimal points, then add like normal decimals - paise under paise.
